coredata.dc:title: Ethanol recognition based on carbon quantum dots sensitized Ti3C2Tx MXene and its enhancement effect of ultraviolet condition under low temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Rusen
Wang, Qi
Song, Peng
coredata.dc:description: The deteriorating air quality makes it particularly important to detect all kinds of harmful gases in the air. In this study, the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite was formed by modifying carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene, in which the Ti3C2Tx MXene sensitized by CQDs achieved enhanced recognition of ethanol. After a series of characterizations, it was confirmed that CQDs were indeed modified on the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The gas sensing test results show that the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits excellent response performance to ethanol, particularly achieving a high response value of 15.38–50 ppm ethanol at the optimal operating temperature of 140 °C. Furthermore, this composite also demonstrates excellent repeatability and a stable response relationship towards ethanol. Finally, it is found by comparison that the recovery time of the sensor under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is significantly shortened, which further verifies that the sensor has wider application potential under UV condition. The experimental results show that the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite significantly improves the efficiency and ability of detecting ethanol by optimization of photoresponsive performance, enhancement of electrical conductivity, and increase in specific surface area. This paper provides practical research methods and ideas for the development of novel sensors based on CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ti3C2Tx MXene
Ethanol detection
Gas sensor
Photoresponse

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Ti3C2Tx MXene, CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The flow chart of the preparation of sensing materials and schematic diagram of the composition of the sensor.
XRD patterns of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite.
SEM images of (a–b) Pure Ti3C2Tx MXene, (c–d) Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite.
TEM images and HRTEM images of (a–c) Pure CQDs, (d–f) Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite.

XPS diagram of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite: (a) Total spectrum, (b) Ti 2p spectrum of Ti3C2Tx MXene, (c) Ti 2p spectrum of composite, (d) O 1s spectrum of Ti3C2Tx MXene, (e) O 1s spectrum of composite; (f) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves and pore size distribution (inset) of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite.

Gas sensing tests: (a) Optimum operating temperature of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite towards ethanol; (b) Response of sensors based on pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to different gases (50 ppm) at 140 °C; Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite at 140 °C: (c) The three-cycle dynamic response curve to 50 ppm ethanol, (d) The dynamic response curve of resistance value and response/recovery time to 50 ppm ethanol, (e) Concentration gradient (10–1000 ppm) test towards ethanol, (f) Linear fit of gas concentration and sensitivity.

Gas sensing tests: (a) Response of materials to 50 ppm ethanol under three conditions in the temperature range of 0–100 °C; Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under UV irradiation condition at 100 °C: (b) The dynamic response curve and response/recovery time of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to 50 ppm ethanol, (c) The three-cycle dynamic response curve to 50 ppm ethanol, (d) Concentration gradient (10–1000 ppm) test towards ethanol.
Schematic diagram for the gas-sensing mechanism of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under different conditions.
Comparison of sensor performance based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite with other sensors related with Ti3C2Tx MXene.

head.ce:title: Ethanol recognition based on carbon quantum dots sensitized Ti3C2Tx MXene and its enhancement effect of ultraviolet condition under low temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The deteriorating air quality makes it particularly important to detect all kinds of harmful gases in the air. In this study, the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite was formed by modifying carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene, in which the Ti3C2Tx MXene sensitized by CQDs achieved enhanced recognition of ethanol. After a series of characterizations, it was confirmed that CQDs were indeed modified on the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The gas sensing test results show that the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits excellent response performance to ethanol, particularly achieving a high response value of 15.38–50 ppm ethanol at the optimal operating temperature of 140 °C. Furthermore, this composite also demonstrates excellent repeatability and a stable response relationship towards ethanol. Finally, it is found by comparison that the recovery time of the sensor under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is significantly shortened, which further verifies that the sensor has wider application potential under UV condition. The experimental results show that the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite significantly improves the efficiency and ability of detecting ethanol by optimization of photoresponsive performance, enhancement of electrical conductivity, and increase in specific surface area. This paper provides practical research methods and ideas for the development of novel sensors based on CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution refers to the increase of the concentration of pollutants in the atmosphere to the extent that it endangers the environment or biological health, and may even destroy the ecosystem and affect the basic life and development of human beings, thus causing harm to organisms [1]. The causes of air pollution can be broadly categorized into two types: natural causes (such as volcanic eruptions, forest disasters, and rock weathering) and man-made causes (such as such as industrial emissions, combustion of fuels, vehicle exhaust, and nuclear explosions), among which the impact of human causes on the atmosphere is far greater than that of natural causes [2,3]. Among the waste produced by industrial production, the emission of waste gas is the largest, and the air pollution is also the most serious [4,5]. In addition to industrial emissions, automobile exhaust emissions are also an important factor causing air pollution [6,7]. This leads to increasing air pollution and air quality is deteriorating, but for humans and the vast majority of plants, animals and plants, air is an indispensable condition for survival. Therefore, the detection of air quality is particularly important, so gas sensors came into being, and we need to develop gas sensors with highly stable performance, sensitive response and as low price as possible [8]. In the field of gas sensing, the sensors based on metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) have attracted the attention and favor of many scientists due to their advantages of easy manufacturing, convenient portability, high gas sensing testing, low cost, and long working life [9,10]. In essence, its sensing mechanism is the redox reaction between the measured gas molecules and the sensing material, which causes the change of the resistance value of the sensing material [11–13]. However, the optimal working temperature of pure MOS (such as indium oxide [14], molybdenum oxide [15] and iron oxide [16]) is more than 200 °C, which is a bottleneck problem at the present stage of the development of gas sensor. This is because high temperature will accelerate the aging and oxidation process of the materials in the sensor, resulting in shorter sensor life, and high temperature means high power consumption, which is not conducive to green development [17–19]. Therefore, it is particularly important to seek low-temperature sensors.

In recent years, the two-dimensional (2D) material Ti3C2Tx MXene has attracted wide attention to because of its large surface area and rich functional groups, which can show excellent gas-sensing properties at low temperatures [20,21]. As a new two-dimensional layered structural material, Ti3C2Tx MXene includes transition metal carbides, nitrides and carbon nitrides [22]. Due to its layered structure, the electrons in Ti3C2Tx MXene are bound and can only move in two dimensions, which gives Ti3C2Tx MXene a larger surface area. Due to the layered structure of Ti3C2Tx MXene, electrons are bound and can only move in two dimensions, resulting in a larger surface area. In addition, carrier and thermal diffusion are confined within its two-dimensional plane, which results in Ti3C2Tx MXene having many advantages such as high carrier mobility, electrical conductivity, and thermal conductivity. This makes Ti3C2Tx MXene potentially valuable for applications in energy storage, biomedicine, photocatalysis/electrocatalysis, environmental science, and sensing [23–25]. However, the gas sensing response value of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene is relatively low [26], so developing composites related to Ti3C2Tx MXene is a feasible solution to solve the above problems. Considering the large surface area of Ti3C2Tx MXene, constructing heterojunctions is an effective strategy [27–29]. At the same time, in order to maximize the utilization of this advantage, the size of the material combined with it should not be too large [30].

Therefore, choosing the zero-dimensional (0D) material for recombination is theoretically a very feasible choice. 0D materials, also known as nanomaterials, refer to materials that have very small dimensions in all three dimensions and therefore do not possess the three-dimensional scalability and volume of traditional materials. In general, 0D materials include quantum dots, nanoparticles, nanopores, and metal particles [31]. Due to their nanoscale size and special quantum effects, these materials exhibit many properties and potential applications different from those of conventional materials, such as highly sensitive sensors, efficient catalysts, and novel electronic devices [32,33]. In the above material, quantum dots have been introduced into the field of gas sensing by virtue of their nanoscale size, quantum size effect, and high sensitivity, which allows them to respond to very low concentrations of gas molecules in the environment [34,35]. Among many quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a typical material with excellent chemical and thermal stability, as well as naturally environmentally friendly and sensitive to the adsorption and desorption processes of gas molecules, and thus have a certain potential for gas sensing [36–38]. Therefore, we tried to construct Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to design more efficient gas sensors by utilizing the high surface area and surface chemical activity of Ti3C2Tx MXene and the optoelectronic properties and high sensitivity of CQDs.

In this study, Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs were first successfully synthesized using hydrofluoric acid etching and hydrothermal method. Subsequently, we modified CQDs onto the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene to form Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite. A series of characterizations verified that the CQDs were effectively attached to the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The gas sensing test results show that the sensor based on the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits excellent response performance to ethanol, especially at the optimal operating temperature of 140 °C, with a response value of 15.38–50 ppm ethanol. In addition, the composite exhibits excellent reproducibility and a stable response change relationship. Finally, we compared the gas sensing performance under sunlight and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation conditions and found that the recovery time of the sensor under UV irradiation was significantly shorter, which further validates its potential for wider applications under UV condition.

The raw materials used include hydrofluoric acid (HF, 10 wt%), titanium aluminium carbon (Ti3AlC2, purity ≥ 98.0 %), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, purity ≥ 99.0 %) and L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, purity ≥ 90.0 %). All materials are analytically pure grade without additional processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ti3C2Tx MXene, CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Ti3C2Tx MXene: First, 10 mL of HF was removed from the fume hood and transferred to a plastic beaker. Subsequently, 5.14 mmol of Ti3AlC2 was added slowly and gradually to the beaker containing HF, with small amounts of Ti3AlC2 being added several times and lightly stirred during the above process. The Ti3C2Tx MXene suspension was prepared by placing the above mixture into a thermostatic water bath at 60 °C for 8 h with stirring and heating. The prepared Ti3C2Tx MXene suspension was then centrifuged at 3000 rpm/min for 5 min to obtain a black precipitate. The resulting clay-like black precipitate was subsequently washed and centrifuged repeatedly with deionized (DI) water until the pH of the supernatant was tested to be ≥ 6 or close to 6.5. After centrifugation, the precipitate was transferred to a petri dish for drying and collection. The washed and dried black precipitate was collected as the prepared Ti3C2Tx MXene.

CQDs: Firstly, 6 mL of DI water was taken and poured into a glass beaker, followed by 4 mL of ethylene glycol was pipetted using a pipette gun and added to the same beaker. Next, 2.27 mmol of L-ascorbic acid was weighed and added to the beaker to mix with the above liquid. After stirring the mixed solution for 30 min, it was transferred to a 50 mL reactor and heated at 160 °C for 70 min. Finally, 1 wt % yellow liquid CQDs were obtained after cooling to room temperature.

Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite: 0.26 mmol of Ti3C2Tx MXene powder was first placed in a glass beaker, followed by 10 mL of DI water. The solution was sonicated using an ultrasonic device for 20 min to ensure adequate dispersion. Next, 52 μL of liquid CQDs were added to the glass beaker and stirred at room temperature for 3 h. After completing the stirring, the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min, followed by two washes with DI water, and finally the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite sample was preserved in liquid form. Fig. 1 shows the preparation process of all the above materials as well as the schematic composition of the sensor.

To analyze the constituent phases of the samples, Cu Kα radiation in X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) was used. Surface properties and morphology were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA4), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai F20). The surface chemical states and valence states of the materials of interest were investigated using advanced X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Kalpha) techniques. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the composite was analyzed using nitrogen (N2) adsorption desorption test (BET, Micromeritics 3Flex).

The gas sensor tests covered in the article were all performed using the WS-30B system (Weisheng, Zhengzhou, China). The composition of the gas sensor is shown in Fig. 1, including two parts: the base and the sensor element. Prior to testing, the prepared sensing material was coated to form a thin film on the sensing element. Subsequently, the sensor was placed in an 18 L glass chamber for gas-sensitive performance testing. The concentration of the volatile organic compounds (VOC) gas was obtained by injecting a corresponding volume of liquid for evaporation, which can be calculated by the following equation:
(1)
C =
24.5 × ρ × d ×
V 1
M ×
V 2
Where C is the concentration of the target gas, ρ denotes the density of the target gas, d represents the volume fraction of the target gas, V1 signifies the volume of the target gas, V2 represents the volume of the chamber (18 L), and M stands for the molecular weight of the target gas. The response value in the gas sensing test is calculated by the following equation:
(2)
R e s p o n s e =
R a
−
R g
R g
× 100 %
Here, Ra and Rg denote the resistance values of the sensing material in ambient and test gas environments, respectively. The response time (τres) and recovery time (τrec) are defined as the durations for the sensor resistance to reach 90 % of its steady-state value and return to baseline, respectively.

The physical phase composition and crystal structure of the samples were determined by XRD analysis. From Fig. 2 , it can be observed that the positions and intensities of the characteristic diffraction peaks of Ti3C2Tx MXene are highly consistent with those of its standard card (PDF#52–0875). Specifically, the diffraction peaks of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene at 2θ = 9.265°, 18.687°, 36.441°, 41.797°, and 60.543° correspond to (0 0 2), (0 0 4), (1 0 2), (1 0 5), and (1 1 0) crystal planes, respectively, which indicates that it was successfully prepared and had a high purity [39]. Comparison of the data of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite with that of the pure Ti3C2Tx MXene sample reveals that their profiles are essentially the same, with the peak positions and intensities remaining roughly the same. The reasons for this phenomenon can be attributed to the following two points: firstly, the amount of CQDs added in the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is relatively small, which has a limited effect on the physical phase composition and crystal structure of the sample. The second is the poor crystalline property of the CQDs themselves [40], which leads to the difficulty in observing the diffraction peaks specific to CQDs in the XRD patterns of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite sample.

From the SEM images of Ti3C2Tx MXene in Fig. 3 (a and b), it can be observed that HF selectively etched the Al layer in Ti3AlC2 after mixing with HF and heating with constant stirring at 60 °C for 8 h. This results in the formation of an accordion-like layer-like structure of MXene. This leads to an increase in the layer spacing of MXene and the formation of an accordion-like layer structure. This structure has a larger surface area than other materials with similar dimensions, which can provide a wider contact space for gas molecules and richer adsorption sites, and thus a unique advantage in the field of gas-sensitive testing [41]. From the SEM images of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite demonstrated in Fig. 3(c and d), it can be seen that the structure of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite does not change much from that of the Ti3C2Tx MXene sample, and the structure of the CQDs is difficult to be distinguished. This is due to the fact that the size of the CQDs is about 10 nm, which is much smaller than that of the Ti3C2Tx MXene of about 10 μm, and thus is not easily observed in the SEM images. However, it can be noticed that the surface of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is slightly rough, which is caused by the attachment of CQDs to the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The above analyses verify the successful preparation of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite by the adopted experimental method.

It can be observed from the TEM images of pure CQDs shown in Fig. 4 (a and b) that the CQDs are spherical, regular in shape, uniform in size and have a diameter of about 10 nm. The lattice stripe spacing in Fig. 4(c) is 0.260 nm, which corresponds to the (0 0 2) crystalline surface of the CQDs, which is consistent with the subsequent analysis in the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite. The lamellar structure of the Ti3C2Tx MXene material can be seen more clearly in Fig. 4(d). However, due to the size of CQDs is much smaller than that of Ti3C2Tx MXene, it is still difficult to see the obvious CQDs image in the TEM image. But a comparison of the lattice stripe spacing reveals the presence of CQDs in the above figure at the position shown in Fig. 4(f). Fig. 4(e and f) shows the lattice stripe spacing of 0.260 nm in the high-resolution transmission diagram corresponds to the (0 0 2) crystalline plane of the CQDs, while the lattice stripe spacing of 1.567 nm corresponds to the (1 1 0) crystalline plane of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The distribution of CQDs in the figure is more dispersed, which is because fewer CQDs are incorporated in the experiment. The successful recombination of Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs is again demonstrated by TEM characterization.

In order to be able to further analyze the elemental composition and chemical composition content between Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite and pure Ti3C2Tx MXene, we performed XPS analysis. The work function of the material (W = 4.4 eV) was used to estimate the bond energy E of C-C bond. The formula W + E = 289.50 ± 0.15 eV was used to obtain the bond energy E = 285.1 eV, and this data was used as calibration data for XPS data fitting analysis [42]. Fig. 5 (a) shows a comparison of the XPS total spectra of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite and the pure Ti3C2Tx MXene. It can be observed that there is not much difference between the XPS total spectra of both except for the peaks of carbon. However, due to the attachment of CQDs, the carbon peak in the total spectra of the composite is higher than that of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene. This further confirms the successful recombination of Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs. It can also be observed that the XPS spectra of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite and Ti3C2Tx MXene contain characteristic peaks of Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s, and the signal peaks of the Al element do not appear, which suggests that we have successfully etched away the Al layer in Ti3AlC2. As shown in Fig. 5(b and c), the Ti 2p spectra of both Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite and Ti3C2Tx MXene spin-split into four peaks. Among them, for Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite, the peaks of Ti 2p3/2 with binding energies located at 453.86 eV, 454.55 eV, and 455.41 eV belong to sub-stoichiometric titanium oxides or carbons Ti-X (Ti2+), low valence titanium ions TixOy (Ti3+), and Ti-O (Ti4+) bonds, respectively. Meanwhile the binding energy of Ti 2p1/2 has a peak at 460.89 eV attributed to Ti-C(Ti+) [43]. Two major binding peaks of O 1s can be observed in Fig. 5(d and e), where the binding energies of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite are located at 528.82 eV and 531.06 eV, corresponding to Ti-O-Ti and Ti-OH bonds, respectively [39]. And it can be seen from the figure that in terms of chemisorbed oxygen (OC) fitted from the rightmost peak of the two materials, the corresponding peak area of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is larger, which means that Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is able to be able to adsorb more oxygen molecules in the environment [44]. The increase of adsorbed oxygen can promote the adsorption and desorption process of gas molecules on the sensor surface, which can enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, and the target gas can be detected quickly and accurately even in a low concentration gas environment [45,46].

The change in OC is mainly through the increase in specific surface area [47,48], therefore the specific surface area of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite was analyzed using N2 adsorption desorption isotherms and BJH pore distribution curves, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(f). After testing, it is concluded that the adsorption and desorption curve of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is type IV curve, and the specific surface area was 3.06 m2/g, which is indeed larger than the specific surface area of Ti3C2Tx MXene reported in the literature (1.40 m2/g), further confirming the above mentioned changes of OC. These results strongly confirm the formation of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite and its potential application in gas sensing.

In order to promote the application of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite in gas sensing, a series of gas-sensitive performance tests were conducted and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . In Fig. 6(a), the response values of Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite are demonstrated in an ethanol environment of 50 ppm over a temperature range of 20–220 °C. The response values of both materials peak at 140 °C, and the maximum response value of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is 15.38, which is significantly higher than that of the pure Ti3C2Tx MXene. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the selectivity of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite for different gases was tested at 140 °C, and it is found that both have the highest response values to ethanol. At the same time, it can be found that compared to the original sample, the response of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to all VOC gases has been improved, with a particularly significant increase in ethanol, far greater than the response values to other VOC gases. This indicates that Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is a highly promising and targeted ethanol sensor. Based on the above analysis, we subsequently conducted a series of sensing properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite at the optimal working temperature of 140 °C. The repeatability test shown in Fig. 6 (c) further validates the stability and consistency of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite as a sensor. Multiple cyclic tests reveal that the response values of the composite show minimal differences after multiple exposures to ethanol gas, further highlighting its excellent response/recovery characteristics. Fig. 6(d) shows the resistance changes of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite before and after contact with 50 ppm ethanol at 140 °C. After being exposed to ethanol gas at 30 s, the resistance of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite rapidly increased, and then decreased again after returning to the air environment. The Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits p-type semiconductor properties as a whole [49], with a response/recovery time of approximately 1/8 s. According to the cyclic dynamic response/recovery curves of the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite for different concentrations (10–1000 ppm) of ethanol gas demonstrated in Fig. 6(e), it can be found that the response of the sensor shows a gradual increase as the ethanol concentration increases. The result of the linear fitting of the sensor response versus ethanol concentration is given in Fig. 6(f) with the fitting equation Y = 0.4499X + 0.3622, where Y denotes the logarithmic response of the sensor and X is the logarithmic concentration of ethanol gas. The fitted data show that the response of the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to ethanol concentration is almost linear (R2 = 0.9422), which is an important guide for determining the ambient ethanol content in practical applications.

Considering also the excellent performance of CQDs under UV conditions, we tested the gas-sensitive properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under UV irradiation condition in the low temperature interval (20–100 °C). As can be seen in Fig. 7 (a), the response values of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under UV irradiation are higher than those of pure Ti3C2Tx MXene and Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under sunlight irradiation throughout the temperature interval, with a maximum response value of 20.71 at 100 °C. This indicates that sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite has more powerful applications under UV condition. Unlike sunlight irradiation, the tests of gas-sensitive performance under UV irradiation conditions were conducted at 100 °C. Fig. 7(b) demonstrates the single-cycle dynamic response curve of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite-based sensor to 50 ppm ethanol at an operating temperature of 100 °C, illustrated by the resistance change. It can be seen that the response/recovery time of the material at 100 °C under UV irradiation is shortened to 1/6 s, which is better than its performance at 140 °C under sunlight irradiation (1/8 s). The reproducibility test in Fig. 7(c) further demonstrates the stability and consistency of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under UV condition. Finally, the results of the concentration gradient test (10–1000 ppm) under UV condition, demonstrated in Fig. 7(d), show that the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite also has a good linearity in response to ethanol, which further validates their ability to be applied as sensors under UV condition. Meanwhile, we compared the sensors based on the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite with the previously reported Ti3C2Tx MXene-based sensors, as shown in Table 1 [20,50–54]. Based on the data, the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits higher response value and shorter response/recovery time at low concentrations. Therefore, we believe that Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite has significant potential in the field of gas sensing.

According to the gas sensing test results in Fig. 6, it is determined that the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits classic p-type sensing behavior, which mainly changes the material resistance value through changes in the number of holes during the sensing process [28]. In the air environment, the free oxygen will adsorb on the surface of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite material, which will capture the electrons of the material to generate the negative oxygen ions (O2 −, O−, O2−) and form a hole accumulation layer on the surface of the material. This process leads to an increase in the number of holes in the sensing layer material and the carrier concentration, which leads to the low resistance state. When exposed to an ethanol atmosphere, the gas molecules will react with the oxygen negative ions on the surface of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to generate CO2 and H2O. This reaction releases the trapped electrons back to the surface of the sensing material and undergo electron-hole recombination, leading to a thinning of the hole accumulation layer and thus a significant increase in the overall resistance of the material. When restored to the air environment, O2 will re-adsorb on the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite, and the resistance value will return to the original state, completing the whole response recovery process [55,56]. The reaction processes involved as described above are as follows:
(3)
O 2
( gas )
→
O 2
( ads )
(4)
O 2
( ads )
+
e ‐
→
O 2 ‐
( ads )
(5)
O 2 ‐
( ads )
+ e
‐
→ 2 O
‐
(6)
C 2
H 5
OH
( gas )
→
C 2
H 5
OH
( ads )
(7)
C 2
H 5
OH
( ads )
+
O ‐
→
H 2
O
( gas )
+
CO 2
( gas )
+
e ‐

The above analysis of the gas-sensing performance shows that the properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is significantly improved compared with pure Ti3C2Tx MXene. The reasons include the following aspects:

The first point is that CQDs have very excellent photoresponsive properties, which is attributed to their quantum size effect and modulation of energy band structure. CQDs have a wide range of light-absorbing ability, which can effectively absorb light energy and convert it into energy for the material to react with the gas, thus facilitating the gas sensing process. Ti3C2Tx MXene, as a matrix, provides a stable support structure and surface active sites, which is conducive to the uniform dispersion of CQDs and maximizes the light-absorbing surface area. When the CQDs are modified on the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene, they are able to co-absorb a wider range of spectra, improving the efficiency of the material's use of light. Therefore, the interaction between the composite CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene can further enhance the light absorption [57]. Meanwhile, the introduction of CQDs enhances the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite. Under light, CQDs may promote the separation of photogenerated carriers by acting at the interface with Ti3C2Tx MXene. This separation effect can increase the effective utilization of electrons and holes, thus enhancing the response speed and sensitivity of the gas sensor. In the same time, these photoactivated electron-hole pairs can participate in the chemical reaction of the material surface, and then affect the gas adsorption and release process, thus enhancing the gas-sensitive properties. Finally, photoactivated CQDs may catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species on Ti3C2Tx MXene surface, such as hydroxyl radicals or superoxide ions, to facilitate the proceeding of gas reaction dynamics [58,59].

Secondly, the overall electrical conductivity of the material is enhanced by the combination of CQDs with Ti3C2Tx MXene. The CQDs themselves have good conductive properties and can act as a transport channel for electrons. When the CQDs are compounded with Ti3C2Tx MXene, an ordered electron transport network is formed between the CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene, which effectively promotes the rapid transport of electrons within the material, thus reducing the resistance of electron transport and improving the electronic conductivity of the material. Moreover, the interface effect of carbon quantum dots combined with Ti3C2Tx MXene will promote electron transfer and transmission. Specifically, the CQDs can fill the gaps and defects between the Ti3C2Tx MXene layers after binding to form a more continuous and orderly conductive pathway. This optimization can promote the migration and diffusion of electrons in the energy band, enabling electrons to respond to external gas stimuli more quickly, and improving the response speed and sensitivity of the sensor [60]. Meanwhile, the binding loss of electron and hole is an important influencing factor. By introducing CQDs, an interface capable of effectively separating electrons and holes can be formed on the Ti3C2Tx MXene surface. This interface helps to reduce the recombination of electrons and holes, thus further improving the electron transmission efficiency and sensitivity of the sensor [61]. At last, the introduction of CQDs alters the surface charge distribution and energy band structure of Ti3C2Tx MXene, enabling more efficient injection and recycling of electrons at the interface. This increase in charge transfer efficiency not only enhances the response sensitivity of the sensor, but also improves its stability and recyclability [62].

The third point is the increase in the specific surface area of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite mentioned earlier. Increasing specific surface area means more active sites on the material surface, which are able to adsorb surrounding gas molecules more efficiently. This increase can improve the detection sensitivity and response speed of the sensor to the gas, as more adsorption sites mean that more gas molecules are trapped and detected [63]. The increased specific surface area of the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite also enhances the chemical reactivity of their surfaces and increases the diffusion rate of gas molecules across the material surface. This means that gas molecules can enter or leave the surface of the material more quickly, resulting in faster sensor response time and recovery time. Simultaneously increased specific surface area is usually accompanied by more microscopic pores and channels that improve the diffusion rate and path of gas molecules within the material. This improvement can allow gas molecules to reach the adsorption sites more quickly and diffuse out of the material more rapidly [64–66].

Compared to sunlight exposure, Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite shows stronger gas-sensing performance under UV light exposure. This is due to the fact that UV light has a higher energy than visible light, enabling the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite to absorb the light energy more efficiently, which allows the photovoltaic properties of CQDs to be further exploited. For example, UV light can more effectively excite the charge transfer process on the surface of the sensing material, thereby accelerating the generation and transmission of the sensing signal and enabling more sensitive and instantaneous detection of changes in gas concentration. That is, the enhancement of gas sensing ability of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite under UV irradiation condition is still mainly achieved by the excellent photoelectric properties of CQDs [67,68].

In conclusion, the enhanced gas-sensitive properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composites can be attributed to three key factors. Firstly, the synergism of CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene significantly enhanced the photoresponsive properties of the material. The CQDs enhance the light absorption ability of the material through its quantum size effect and interaction with Ti3C2Tx MXene, which enhance the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers, thereby improving the responsiveness and sensitivity of the sensor to the gas. Next, the improved electrical conductivity of the composite is attributed to the fact that the CQDs act as effective electron transport channels, optimizing the electron transport paths within the material as well as the charge distribution of the material, reducing the resistance to electron transport in the sensor response. Finally, the gas sensitivity performance of the sensor based on Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite is improved due to the increase of specific surface area. The increased specific surface area increases the number of active sites, accelerates the adsorption and diffusion rate of gas molecules on the surface of the material, and thus improves the detection sensitivity and response speed of the sensor. The corresponding gas sensing process and the reasons for the improvement of the gas-sensitive performance are shown in Fig. 8 .

In this study, Ti3C2Tx MXene and CQDs were successfully synthesized by hydrofluoric acid etching and hydrothermal methods. Subsequently, the CQDs were modified onto the surface of Ti3C2Tx MXene to form Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite, in which enhanced recognition of ethanol gas was achieved by sensitizing Ti3C2Tx MXene with CQDs. The gas sensing test results show that the sensor based on the Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite exhibits excellent response performance to ethanol, in particular, a response value as high as 15.38–50 ppm ethanol at the optimal operating temperature of 140 °C. In addition, the composite exhibits excellent reproducibility and a stable response variation relationship. Finally, we compared the gas sensing performance under sunlight and UV irradiation conditions and found that the recovery time of the sensor under UV irradiation was significantly shortened, which further verified its potential for wider application under UV condition. The Ti3C2Tx MXene/CQDs composite has significantly improved the efficiency and detection of ethanol gases in the ambient environment through the optimization of photoresponse properties, enhancement of electrical conductivity, and the increase of specific surface area. This paper provides practical research methods and ideas for the development of effective sensors based on CQDs and Ti3C2Tx MXene.
Rusen Tian: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Qi Wang: Validation, Resources. Peng Song: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61102006), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2022MF234 and No. ZR2018LE006).
